6	def human(n):
7	    if n >= 1e12: return f"{n/1e12:.2f}T"
8	    if n >= 1e9:  return f"{n/1e9:.2f}B"
9	    if n >= 1e6:  return f"{n/1e6:.2f}M"
10	    if n >= 1e3:  return f"{n/1e3:.2f}K"
11	    return f"{n:.1f}"
12	
13	SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
14	SEC_PER_MONTH = 30 * SEC_PER_DAY
15	
16	# ---- Fleet assumptions (locked in scope) ----
17	MACHINES = 200_000          # "thousands" -> we size for a 200K-machine fleet ceiling
18	HEARTBEAT_SEC = 60          # one telemetry heartbeat per machine per minute
19	EVENT_BYTES = 400           # one telemetry sample on the wire (compact JSON / protobuf-ish)
20	ROW_BYTES = 250             # one stored time-series sample (typed columns, compressed-ish before TSDB)
21	REPL = 3                    # replication factor for the time-series store
22	TSDB_COMPRESSION = 10       # columnar TSDB compresses ~10x on repetitive telemetry

32	print(f"  -> talkable: average ~{round(avg_qps/1000)}K msgs/s, peak ~{round(peak_qps/1000)}K msgs/s")
33

36	print("=" * 70)
37	ingest_bw_avg = avg_qps * EVENT_BYTES

39	print(f"  avg {avg_qps:,.0f} msgs/s x {EVENT_BYTES}B = {human(ingest_bw_avg)}B/s = ~{ingest_bw_avg*8/1e6:.0f} Mbps")
40	print(f"  peak {peak_qps:,.0f} msgs/s x {EVENT_BYTES}B = {human(ingest_bw_peak)}B/s = ~{ingest_bw_peak*8/1e6:.0f} Mbps")
41	
42	print("=" * 70)
43	print("3. TIME-SERIES STORAGE FOOTPRINT (largest table)")
44	print("=" * 70)
45	samples_per_day = avg_qps * SEC_PER_DAY
46	samples_per_month = avg_qps * SEC_PER_MONTH
47	print(f"  samples/day  = {avg_qps:,.0f}/s x {SEC_PER_DAY:,}s = {human(samples_per_day)} rows/day")
48	print(f"  samples/month = {human(samples_per_month)} rows/month")

52	print(f"  after {TSDB_COMPRESSION}x TSDB compression x{REPL} replicas = {human(stored_month)}B/month stored")
53	RETENTION_MONTHS = 13  # 13 months hot so billing + YoY comparisons work
54	hot_total = stored_month * RETENTION_MONTHS
55	print(f"  {RETENTION_MONTHS}-month hot retention -> {human(hot_total)}B resident")
56	COST_PER_GB_MONTH = 0.10  # blended SSD-backed TSDB $/GB-month
57	hot_gb = hot_total / 1e9

59	print(f"  ~{human(hot_total)}B = {hot_gb:,.0f} GB x ${COST_PER_GB_MONTH}/GB-mo = ~${monthly_storage_cost:,.0f}/mo storage")
60	
61	print("=" * 70)
62	print("4. STATE READS (dashboards + alert engine) sized from MISS QPS")
63	print("=" * 70)
64	# Latest-state reads: dashboards poll, ops query, alert engine evaluates rules.
65	DASH_USERS = 2_000          # ops + lessee dashboard concurrent viewers
66	DASH_POLL_SEC = 10          # each open dashboard refreshes a tile set every 10s

69	print(f"  {DASH_USERS} viewers x {TILES_PER_REFRESH} tiles / {DASH_POLL_SEC}s = {dash_read_qps:,.0f} read QPS")
70	HIT_RATIO = 0.95            # latest-state cache (Redis) absorbs hot reads

72	print(f"  with {HIT_RATIO:.0%} cache hit -> miss QPS to store = {miss_qps:,.0f}/s")

90	ingest_compute = 8_000      # gateway + stream brokers + ingest workers
91	tsdb_cost = monthly_storage_cost + 6_000  # storage + TSDB query nodes
92	other = 6_000               # dashboards, billing, alerting, observability
93	monthly_infra = ingest_compute + tsdb_cost + other
94	print(f"  monthly infra ~= ingest ${ingest_compute:,} + tsdb ${tsdb_cost:,.0f} + other ${other:,} = ~${monthly_infra:,.0f}/mo")
95	cost_per_million = monthly_infra / (msgs_per_month / 1e6)
96	print(f"  msgs/month = {human(msgs_per_month)}")
97	print(f"  ${monthly_infra:,.0f} / {msgs_per_month/1e6:,.0f}M msgs = ~${cost_per_million:.3f} per 1M telemetry msgs")
98	
99	print("=" * 70)
100	print("ROUNDED TALKABLE SUMMARY")
101	print("=" * 70)
102	print(f"  ingest avg     ~ {round(avg_qps/1000)}K msgs/s")
103	print(f"  ingest peak    ~ {round(peak_qps/1000)}K msgs/s  (x{BURST} reconnect storm)")
104	print(f"  TSDB write     ~ {round(avg_qps/1000)}K rows/s sustained")
105	print(f"  storage hot    ~ {round(hot_total/1e12)} TB  ({RETENTION_MONTHS} mo, x{REPL} repl, {TSDB_COMPRESSION}x compress)")
106	print(f"  storage $      ~ ${round(monthly_storage_cost/1000)}K/mo")
107	print(f"  dashboard read ~ {round(dash_read_qps/1000)}K QPS, miss ~ {round(miss_qps)} /s")
108	print(f"  infra total    ~ ${round(monthly_infra/1000)}K/mo")
109	print(f"  unit econ      ~ ${cost_per_million:.2f} per 1M msgs")

1	# Design a Vending Machine Leasing Management System (HLD)
2	
3	We're designing the fleet-scale backend that leases vending machines to operators: every machine phones home with telemetry (stock levels, cash box, fault codes), we bill the lessee, we dispatch restock and repair crews when stock runs low or a machine faults, and we light up dashboards for both our ops team and the lessees. The core tension: thousands of flaky IoT devices on cellular links produce an endless firehose of time-stamped state, and we have to turn that noisy stream into *money* (correct lease bills) and *action* (timely restock/repair) without ever blocking the firehose or losing a fault that strands a machine.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the telemetry firehose, the time-series store, and offline-machine handling — checking in with you as I go. I'll keep cost and on-call operations first-class since this is a leased-hardware business where a stranded machine is lost revenue. Sound good?"
10	
11	The one-line frame: **this is an IoT ingestion + time-series + billing system, not a request-response web app** — the hard part is that the *write* path (telemetry) is the firehose, and the *read* path (dashboards) is comparatively tiny. Most of our budget goes to ingest durability and the time-series store.
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)
14	
15	1. **Fleet size is "thousands" — I'll size for a 200K-machine ceiling, single cloud region with multi-AZ.** Telemetry is regional; we don't need multi-region writes. *(If you mean millions of machines or truly global, the ingest tier shards by region and we revisit.)*
16	2. **Machines push a heartbeat roughly once a minute, plus event-driven messages on a vend or a fault.** This makes ingest *steady and predictable*, not spiky like consumer traffic. *(If it's sub-second high-frequency telemetry, the firehose is 60x bigger and dominates everything.)*
17	3. **Billing is lease-based (a fixed monthly fee per machine) plus optional usage metering (a cut of vends).** So billing is a *nightly batch* over aggregated telemetry, not a real-time hot path. *(If billing must be real-time per-vend, it becomes a transactional money path and changes the design.)*
18	4. **Telemetry is "at-least-once, eventually consistent" for dashboards, but faults and cash/vend events feed money and safety, so those must not be silently dropped.** *(If everything needs exactly-once, ingest cost roughly doubles.)*
19	
20	> 💬 "Here's what I'm treating as in scope and out of scope — add or cut before I commit."
21	
22	**Since we're assuming a steady ~per-minute heartbeat from a fixed fleet, the core flows are:**
23	
24	**Functional requirements (above the line):**
25	
26	1. **Ingest telemetry from machines.** Machines send stock, cash, and fault state.
27	   - 🗣️ Plain words: every machine calls home about once a minute saying "I have 12 cokes left, my cash box has $84, and door sensor is fine" — and we have to swallow all of it without dropping anything.
28	   - ⚡ Why it's hard: thousands of devices on flaky cellular links means duplicate, late, and out-of-order messages — and we can never let a slow downstream block the firehose.
29	
30	2. **Store and query machine state over time (time-series).** Keep history for trends, billing, and debugging.
31	   - 🗣️ Plain words: we keep a timeline for each machine so we can answer "how much did stock drop over the last week" and "was this machine offline yesterday."
32	   - ⚡ Why it's hard: ~8.6 billion samples a month — a normal SQL table chokes on that; we need a store built for time-stamped data.
33	
34	3. **Bill lessees (lease + usage).** Generate a monthly invoice per lease.
35	   - 🗣️ Plain words: each operator pays a monthly fee per machine plus a slice of sales, and we have to compute that exactly from the telemetry.
36	   - ⚡ Why it's hard: billing is *money* — it must be exact and idempotent (re-running a billing job can't double-charge), and it depends on telemetry that may arrive late.
37	
38	4. **Detect low-stock / faults and dispatch restock or repair.** Turn telemetry into work orders and alerts.
39	   - 🗣️ Plain words: when a machine is almost empty or its fridge fails, we automatically create a ticket and ping the right crew.
40	   - ⚡ Why it's hard: we must alert fast on real problems but not flood on-call with false alarms from one noisy sensor or a brief network blip.
41	
42	5. **Dashboards for ops and lessees.** Show live fleet health and per-machine detail.
43	   - 🗣️ Plain words: an ops person sees a map of which machines are healthy, low, or down; a lessee sees their machines and their bill.
44	   - ⚡ Why it's hard: the live tile reads must be fast and cheap even though they sit on top of a massive time-series store.
45	
46	**Below the line (out of scope for today):** consumer-facing payment at the machine (assume the machine handles card swipes locally and just reports the total), inventory procurement/supply-chain, machine firmware OTA updates, route optimization for restock trucks, multi-region active-active. I'll mention where each would attach.
47	
48	**Non-functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to assumptions):**
49	
50	- **Ingest must never drop a fault or vend event — at-least-once durability, ingest accept p99 under ~50ms.** (Assumes per-minute heartbeat scale.)
51	  - 🗣️ Plain words: when a machine sends "fridge failed," we guarantee that message lands in durable storage even if everything downstream is on fire.
52	  - 💥 What breaks without it: a stranded machine sits broken for days, the lessee loses sales and trust, and we lose the lease.
53	- **Telemetry-to-dashboard freshness within ~1 minute; dashboard read p99 under ~200ms.** (Assumes single-region, eventual consistency for display.)
54	  - 🗣️ Plain words: what you see on the dashboard is at most about a minute behind reality.
55	  - 💥 What breaks without it: ops chases ghosts — a machine shows "full" long after it's empty.
56	- **Billing correctness: exactly-once invoice per lease per cycle, fully reconcilable.** (Assumes batch billing.)
57	  - 🗣️ Plain words: each operator gets exactly one correct bill a month, and we can always show our math.
58	  - 💥 What breaks without it: double-charges, refunds, chargebacks, and a furious B2B customer.
59	- **Availability: ingest path 99.95% (~4.4 hrs/yr down), dashboard 99.9% (~8.7 hrs/yr).**
60	  - 🗣️ Plain words: the firehose almost never closes; dashboards can blink briefly.
61	  - 💥 What breaks without it: a long ingest outage means a hole in every bill and missed faults.
62	
63	> 💬 "The interesting tension here is that the writes are the firehose and the reads are tiny — opposite of a normal web app — so I'll spend my design budget on ingest durability and the time-series store, and keep the read path deliberately simple."
64	
65	> 🎙️ Script: "So we ingest a per-minute heartbeat plus events from up to 200,000 leased machines, store that as time-series, bill each lease monthly from it, turn low-stock and faults into dispatched work, and show it all on dashboards. The non-negotiables are: never drop a fault or a vend, bill exactly once, and keep dashboards roughly a minute fresh. Everything else I'll treat as eventual and cheap."
66	
67	---
68	
69	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
70	
71	These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the assumptions where being wrong would reshape the design.
72	
73	**Fork 1 — Heartbeat frequency.**
74	- The assumption I locked: machines beat about once a minute (~3K msgs/s average across the fleet).
75	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
76	  - If it's actually **sub-second high-frequency** telemetry → the firehose is 60x bigger (~200K msgs/s), the time-series store becomes the dominant cost, and I'd push edge aggregation onto the device or a regional collector before ingest.
77	  - If it's actually **far slower** (every 15 min) → ingest is trivial, I drop the streaming buffer and just batch-write directly to the TSDB.
78	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm assuming roughly per-minute heartbeats — if your machines stream much faster, tell me and I'll add edge aggregation so we don't store 60x the data."
79	
80	**Fork 2 — Is billing real-time or batch?**
81	- The assumption I locked: billing is monthly batch (lease fee + usage rollup), not per-vend real-time.
82	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
83	  - If billing must be **real-time per vend** → vend events become a money path needing strong consistency and idempotent transactional writes, and I'd add a ledger service with exactly-once semantics off the event stream.
84	  - If billing is **flat-fee only** (no usage) → I can bill straight from the lease table and barely touch telemetry; the rollup pipeline disappears.
85	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm treating billing as a nightly batch over aggregated telemetry — if you need real-time per-sale billing, that becomes a transactional ledger and I'll design that path strongly consistent."
86	
87	**Fork 3 — Offline machine: is silence a fault?**
88	- The assumption I locked: a machine going silent is itself a signal we must detect and act on (not just absence of data).
89	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
90	  - If **silence is benign** (machines sleep) → I drop the heartbeat-watchdog and don't page on missing beats.
91	  - If **silence means lost revenue/safety** (the common case) → I need an active liveness detector (a watchdog that fires when an expected heartbeat doesn't arrive), which is a non-trivial deep dive because "absence of an event" is hard to build on a system that's event-driven.
92	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm assuming a quiet machine is a problem we must catch — that's actually one of the harder parts, detecting the event that *didn't* happen."
93	
94	> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my three load-bearing assumptions — per-minute heartbeats, batch billing, and silence-is-a-fault. Want to lock those, or redirect any before I size the system?"
95	
96	---
97	
98	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
99	
100	I computed these with a small `capacity.py` script so the arithmetic is exact; I'll surface only the rounded, decision-driving ones.
101	
102	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
103	|---|---|---|---|---|
104	| **Ingest QPS (avg)** | 200K machines ÷ 60s heartbeat = 3,333 msgs/s | ~3K msgs/s | "about three thousand messages a second" | Steady, not spiky — a modest stream tier handles it |
105	| **Ingest QPS (peak)** | 3,333 × 3 (reconnect-storm burst) = 10,000 msgs/s | ~10K msgs/s | "about ten thousand a second in a reconnect storm" | **THE decision-forcing number** — size buffer for this, not the average |
106	| **Ingest bandwidth (peak)** | 10K msgs/s × 400 B = 4 MB/s | ~32 Mbps | "a few dozen megabits" | Tiny — bandwidth is a non-issue |
107	| **TSDB write rate** | = ingest avg, one sample per beat | ~3K rows/s | "three thousand rows a second sustained" | Within a single TSDB cluster's reach |
108	| **Samples/month** | 3,333/s × 2.6M s/mo = 8.64B | ~8.6B/mo | "almost nine billion samples a month" | Forces a real time-series store, not SQL |
109	| **Hot storage footprint** | 8.64B × 250 B ÷ 10 (compress) × 3 (repl) × 13 mo = ~8.4 TB | ~8 TB | "about eight terabytes hot" | Cheap — storage is NOT the cost driver |
110	| **Storage $/mo** | 8,424 GB × $0.10/GB-mo = ~$842 | ~$1K/mo | "around a thousand a month" | Confirms compute > storage |
111	| **Dashboard read QPS** | 2,000 viewers × 20 tiles ÷ 10s poll = 4,000 | ~4K QPS | "about four thousand reads a second" | Small — a cache erases it |
112	| **Dashboard MISS QPS** | 4,000 × (1 − 0.95 hit) = 200/s | ~200/s | "two hundred misses a second" | TSDB barely sees reads; cold-cache worst case is 4K/s |
113	| **Infra cost** | ingest $8K + tsdb $6.8K + other $6K = ~$20.8K | ~$21K/mo | "low twenties of thousands a month" | Compute/ops dominate |
114	| **Unit economics** | $20.8K ÷ 8,640M msgs = $0.0000024 ea | ~$2.41 / 1M msgs | "about two and a half dollars per million messages" | Cost-per-machine sanity check for pricing |
115	
116	Glosses: **QPS** = queries (here, messages) per second. **TSDB** = time-series database, a store optimized for time-stamped data with cheap range scans and heavy compression. **Cache hit ratio** = the fraction of reads answered from fast in-memory storage instead of the database. **Burst factor** = how much higher a short peak runs above the steady average.
117	
118	**The ONE decision-forcing number and its flip threshold:** the **peak ~10K msgs/s during a reconnect storm.** That's small per-machine but it arrives *all at once* when a regional cell tower or power blip drops a slice of the fleet and they all reconnect together. We size the durable ingest buffer for this peak, not the 3K average. **Flip threshold:** if heartbeats go sub-second and average ingest crosses **~50K msgs/s**, a single-region single-cluster ingest tier stops being comfortable and we shard ingest by region and push aggregation to the edge.
119	
120	> 🎙️ Script: "The number that matters is the peak — about ten thousand messages a second when a chunk of the fleet reconnects at once after a network blip. Storage is almost a rounding error, about eight terabytes and a thousand dollars a month. So I'm not going to spend effort on storage; I'll spend it on a durable buffer that can absorb that reconnect spike without dropping a single fault."
121

124	## 4. Core Entities
125	
126	This is a first-draft scaffold — full field definitions land in §7.
127	
128	- **Machine** — a physical leased vending machine; owns its identity, hardware config, current firmware, and which lease it belongs to.
129	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like a car in a rental fleet — it has a VIN, an owner-of-record, and a service history.
130	  - ⚠️ Probe: "How do you know a machine is the real machine and not a spoofer?" → each device authenticates with a per-device certificate (mutual TLS); the machine_id is bound to that cert, never trusted from the message body.
131	
132	- **TelemetrySample** — one time-stamped snapshot of a machine's state (stock per slot, cash level, temperature, fault flags).
133	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like a single heartbeat reading on a hospital monitor — one dot on the timeline.
134	  - ⚠️ Probe: "What's your dedup key?" → (machine_id, device_seq) or (machine_id, captured_at) — the device stamps a monotonic sequence number so we drop exact retries.
135	
136	- **Lease** — the contract between us and an operator for one or more machines; owns billing terms (monthly fee, usage rate), start/end dates, and lessee identity.
137	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like an apartment lease — fixed rent plus maybe a utilities cut, with a start and end.
138	  - ⚠️ Probe: "What if a machine moves between leases mid-month?" → telemetry carries the machine_id and timestamp; billing attributes each sample to whichever lease owned the machine at that captured_at.
139	
140	- **Invoice** — the monthly bill computed for a lease; owns the line items, total, status, and the idempotency key for the billing run.
141	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like a monthly credit-card statement — one per cycle, immutable once issued.
142	  - ⚠️ Probe: "How do you avoid double-billing on a retry?" → the invoice is keyed by (lease_id, billing_period); a re-run upserts the same row instead of inserting a new one.
143	
144	- **WorkOrder** — a dispatched task (restock or repair) created from a low-stock or fault signal; owns its type, target machine, assigned crew, and lifecycle status.
145	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like a maintenance ticket — opened automatically, assigned, worked, closed.
146	  - ⚠️ Probe: "How do you stop duplicate tickets for the same flapping fault?" → work orders are deduped on (machine_id, fault_type) while one is open; new signals update, not duplicate.
147	
148	- **Alert / Rule** — a condition (stock < threshold, fault present, machine offline > N minutes) that, when met, produces a WorkOrder or page.
149	  - 🗣️ Analogy: like a thermostat rule — "if temperature crosses X, do Y."
150	
151	> 🎙️ Script: "The entities are Machine, TelemetrySample, Lease, Invoice, WorkOrder, and the Alert rules. The one to watch is TelemetrySample — there are billions of them, so its identity and dedup key drive the whole storage and billing design. In the data model I'll add the full fields, the shard keys, and which store each lives in."
152

154	
155	## 5. API / Interface
156	
157	All endpoints are versioned under `/v1`. The machine-facing ingest API uses mutual-TLS device certs; human-facing APIs use a JWT (a signed token proving who's logged in) and object-level authorization. List endpoints use **cursor pagination** (an opaque token marking where you left off) rather than offset, because under a constantly-growing time-series, offset/limit skips and duplicates rows as data shifts.
158	
159	**5.1 Ingest telemetry — `POST /v1/telemetry`** (device-facing)
160	- Why POST: it creates new immutable state on the server.
161	- Key fields:
162	  - `samples: [...]` — a batch of readings; batching lets a machine flush a few buffered beats in one call after a reconnect.
163	  - `device_seq: int` (per sample) — monotonic counter set by the device; our dedup key, so a retry is harmless.
164	  - `captured_at: timestamp` (per sample) — when the *device* observed the state, not when we received it; matters because messages arrive late.
165	  - The server does NOT trust a `machine_id` from the body — it's bound from the device certificate, so a machine can't impersonate another.
166	```
167	// Request
168	POST /v1/telemetry
169	{ "samples": [
170	  { "device_seq": 88421, "captured_at": "2026-06-21T14:32:00Z",
171	    "stock": {"A1": 3, "A2": 0}, "cash_cents": 8400, "faults": ["COOLING_HIGH"] } ] }
172	
173	// Response 202
174	{ "accepted": 1, "duplicates": 0 }
175	```
176	- Key decision: returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — we durably enqueue and acknowledge fast; processing is async. Idempotency is by (machine_id from cert, device_seq), so the same sample sent twice is counted once.
177	
178	**5.2 Query machine state / history — `GET /v1/machines/{id}/telemetry`**
179	- Why GET: read-only.
180	- Query: `from`, `to`, `metric`, `cursor`. Returns downsampled series for a time range.
181	```
182	GET /v1/machines/m_123/telemetry?metric=stock.A1&from=2026-06-14&to=2026-06-21&cursor=...
183	// Response 200
184	{ "series": [ {"t":"2026-06-14T00:00:00Z","v":12}, {"t":"2026-06-14T01:00:00Z","v":11} ],
185	  "next_cursor": "eyJvZmZzZXQ..." }
186	```
187	- Object-level authz: a lessee JWT can only read machines on their own leases — we check lease ownership, not just "is logged in."
188	
189	**5.3 Get/issue invoice — `GET /v1/leases/{id}/invoices` and `POST /v1/billing/runs`**
190	- `POST /v1/billing/runs` (ops/internal) triggers a billing cycle; `period` field. Idempotency key = `period`, so re-triggering the June run does not create duplicate invoices.
191	```
192	// Request
193	POST /v1/billing/runs
194	{ "period": "2026-06" }
195	// Response 202
196	{ "runId": "run_2026_06", "status": "QUEUED" }
197	```
198	- The client never sends amounts — totals are computed server-side from leases + telemetry rollups, so no one can tamper with a bill.
199	
200	**5.4 Work orders — `POST /v1/workorders` (internal, alert-driven) and `PATCH /v1/workorders/{id}`**
201	- `PATCH` updates status (ASSIGNED → DONE). Why PATCH: partial update of one field.
202	- Idempotency: creation is deduped on (machine_id, fault_type, open) so a flapping fault doesn't spawn duplicates.
203	```
204	// Request
205	PATCH /v1/workorders/wo_55
206	{ "status": "DONE", "crewId": "crew_7" }
207	// Response 200
208	{ "id": "wo_55", "status": "DONE", "closedAt": "2026-06-21T15:10:00Z" }
209	```
210	
211	**5.5 Dashboard fleet view — `GET /v1/fleet/status`**
212	- Returns latest state per machine, filtered/paginated. Backed by the latest-state cache, not the raw TSDB.
213	```
214	GET /v1/fleet/status?leaseId=l_9&state=NEEDS_RESTOCK&cursor=...
215	// Response 200
216	{ "machines": [ {"id":"m_123","state":"NEEDS_RESTOCK","lastSeen":"2026-06-21T14:32:00Z"} ],
217	  "next_cursor": null }
218	```
219	
220	**Versioning:** additive changes (a new optional field) ship without a version bump; breaking changes get `/v2`. **Security recap:** device identity from mTLS cert, human identity from JWT, object-level authz on every read of machine/lease/invoice data, and the server never trusts client-supplied ids, prices, or timestamps that affect money.
221	
222	> 💬 "The one thing I'd call out: ingest returns 202, not 200 — we acknowledge durability, not processing, so a slow downstream never blocks a machine."
223	
224	> 🎙️ Script: "Machines POST batched telemetry and get a fast 202 once it's durably buffered. Humans GET machine history and fleet status through a cache, and they only ever see their own leases. Billing runs are POSTs keyed by period so re-running a month is safe. And critically, the machine never tells us its own id or a bill amount — identity comes from its certificate and money comes from our database."
225

227	
228	## 6. High-Level Design (one flow at a time)
229	
230	I'll define the color language once: blue = devices/clients, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external systems.
231	
232	### 6.1 "Ingest telemetry from machines"
233	
234	The firehose. The whole game is: accept fast, never drop, never let downstream block the device.
235	
236	**Architecture decisions table:**
237	
238	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
239	|---|---|---|---|---|
240	| Ingest Gateway | A thin stateless service that terminates the device's TLS, validates the cert, and writes the batch to a durable log | We need fast 202s and cert auth at the edge; it does almost nothing else so it scales flat | Not writing straight to the TSDB (couples device latency to DB load and loses the buffer); not a fat service (slows the hot accept path) | One extra hop before storage |
241	| Event Log (stream) | Kafka — a durable, ordered log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | Decouples accept from processing; absorbs the 10K/s reconnect spike; lets multiple consumers (TSDB writer, alerting, billing-feed) read independently | Not a plain queue like SQS (consumed-once, can't fan out to billing + alerting + TSDB); not direct DB writes (no buffer, no replay) | Operational cost of running Kafka |
242	| TSDB Writer | A consumer that reads the log and writes samples into the time-series store, deduping by device_seq | Keeps the write to the heavy store off the device's latency path; batches writes for efficiency | Not letting the gateway write (re-couples them) | Telemetry is ~seconds behind real time (fine — freshness target is ~1 min) |
243	
244	> 🎙️ Narration: "The gateway is deliberately dumb — it checks the certificate, drops the batch onto a durable log, and says 202. I'm using Kafka for that log because it's a durable buffer that lets several consumers read the same firehose independently: one writes the time-series store, one runs alerting, one feeds billing. I considered writing straight to the database, but then a slow database would back up onto the machines and we'd lose messages — the log is the shock absorber. The trade-off is we run Kafka and telemetry is a few seconds behind, which is well inside our one-minute freshness goal."
245	
246	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
247	> **Kafka** — a durable append-only log that holds events in order and lets multiple readers consume at their own pace. Here it buffers the telemetry firehose so a slow database or alert engine can never drop a machine's message.
248	> **Ingest Gateway** — a stateless front service that authenticates the device and writes to the log. Here it gives machines a fast, cheap acknowledgement and isolates them from downstream load.
249	> **mTLS (mutual TLS)** — both sides present certificates, so the server knows exactly which machine is connecting. Here it binds machine identity at the edge so the message body can't lie about who it is.
250	> **Consumer** — a process that reads from the log. Here we run several, each doing one job (store, alert, bill).
251	
252	**Telemetry ingest flow:**
253	```mermaid
254	flowchart TD
255	    M[Machine fleet]:::client
256	    M -->|"1 POST batch mTLS"| GW
257	    subgraph Ingest["Ingest accept path"]
258	        GW[Ingest Gateway]:::svc
259	        GW -->|"2 append batch"| K[[Event Log Kafka]]:::async
260	    end
261	    subgraph Consume["Async consumers"]
262	        K -->|"3 read samples"| TW[TSDB Writer]:::svc
263	        K -->|"4 read events"| AL[Alert Engine]:::svc
264	        K -->|"5 read vend events"| BF[Billing Feed]:::svc
265	    end
266	    TW -->|"6 dedup write"| TS[(Time Series Store)]:::store
267	    TW -->|"7 upsert latest"| RS[(Latest State Cache)]:::store
268	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
269	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
270	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
271	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
272	```
273	
274	**Numbered narration:**
275	1. **Machine POSTs a batch over mTLS.** The gateway owns auth because identity must be established before we trust a byte. State: nothing committed yet. What could go wrong: bad cert → 401, machine retries later.
276	2. **Gateway appends the batch to the Kafka log and returns 202.** The log owns durability. State: samples are now durable and replicated. What could go wrong: Kafka unavailable → gateway returns 503, machine buffers locally and retries (machines keep a local ring buffer).
277	3. **TSDB Writer reads samples** because it owns persistence to the time-series store. State: consumer offset advances. What could go wrong: writer crashes → it resumes from its last committed offset, replaying without loss.
278	4. **Alert Engine reads the same events** independently because alerting can't wait on storage. State: rules evaluated. (Detailed in §6.4.)
279	5. **Billing Feed reads vend events** to accumulate usage. State: usage counters updated. (Detailed in §6.3.)
280	6. **TSDB Writer dedups by (machine_id, device_seq) and writes** to the time-series store. State: history persisted. What could go wrong: duplicate sample → dropped by the dedup key.
281	7. **Writer upserts the machine's latest state** into the Latest State Cache so dashboards read fast. State: live view current within seconds.
282	
283	> 💬 "The key move is the durable log between the gateway and everything else — that's what lets us promise 'we never drop a fault' even when the database is slow."
284	
285	> 🎙️ Script: "Machines authenticate with a certificate and POST a batch; the gateway just validates and appends to a Kafka log, then returns a 202. From the log, independent consumers do their jobs — one writes the time-series store and updates a latest-state cache for dashboards, one runs alerting, one feeds billing. The log is the shock absorber that decouples the firehose from the slow parts, so a database hiccup can't lose a machine's message."
286	
287	### 6.2 "Store and query machine state over time"
288	
289	Billions of samples, mostly written once and read in ranges. This is a time-series workload, not relational.
290	
291	**Architecture decisions table:**
292	
293	| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
294	|---|---|---|---|---|
295	| Time-Series Store | A database built for time-stamped data with cheap range scans, automatic time-partitioning, and ~10x compression | We write ~3K rows/s and query by (machine, time range); TSDBs are purpose-built for exactly this and compress repetitive telemetry hard | Not Postgres (8.6B rows/mo bloats indexes, range scans get slow); not S3 raw files (no fast point/range query); not Cassandra (works, but no built-in downsampling/retention) | Less flexible ad-hoc joins than SQL — fine, our access pattern is fixed |
296	| Latest State Cache | Redis — an in-memory key-value store keyed by machine_id holding the most recent state | Dashboards and alerting want "current state of machine X" in sub-ms; reading the TSDB for the latest point is wasteful | Not querying TSDB per tile (200ms+ and load); not a SQL table (slower, more contention) | Cache can be slightly stale or lost — rebuildable from TSDB |
297	| Downsampler | A rollup job that pre-aggregates raw samples into hourly/daily summaries | Dashboards and billing read summaries, not raw points; pre-aggregating makes range reads cheap | Not aggregating on read every time (re-scans billions of rows) | Extra storage for rollups (tiny) and a few minutes of rollup lag |
298	
299	> 🎙️ Narration: "For history I'm using a time-series database — it stores time-stamped rows, partitions them by time automatically, and compresses repetitive telemetry about tenfold, so our eight terabytes is cheap. I rejected Postgres because almost nine billion rows a month would wreck its indexes, and raw files on S3 because you can't query them fast. For the live view I keep each machine's latest state in Redis so a dashboard tile is a sub-millisecond key lookup instead of a database scan. And a downsampler pre-rolls hourly and daily summaries so dashboards and billing never scan raw points."
300	
301	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
302	> **Time-Series Database (TSDB)** — a store optimized for append-mostly, time-stamped data (think InfluxDB/Timescale). Here it holds every machine's history cheaply and answers range queries fast.
303	> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store. Here it caches each machine's current state so dashboards and alert checks are instant.
304	> **Downsampling / rollup** — pre-computing coarser summaries (per hour, per day) from fine-grained points. Here it makes dashboard and billing reads cheap and bounds query cost.
305	
306	**Storage and query paths:**
307	```mermaid
308	flowchart TD
309	    TW[TSDB Writer]:::svc
310	    TW -->|"1 write raw sample"| TS[(Time Series Store)]:::store
311	    TW -->|"2 upsert latest"| RS[(Latest State Cache)]:::store
312	    TS -->|"3 hourly rollup"| DS[Downsampler]:::svc
313	    DS -->|"4 write summaries"| TS
314	    DASH[Dashboard API]:::svc
315	    DASH -->|"5 latest tile read"| RS
316	    DASH -->|"6 history range read"| TS
317	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
318	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
319	```
320	↳ reuses existing: TSDB Writer, Latest State Cache (from §6.1)
321	
322	**Numbered narration:**
323	1. **Writer persists each raw sample** to the time-series store, partitioned by day. State: durable history. Risk: write spike → batched writes smooth it.
324	2. **Writer upserts latest state** to Redis. State: live view current.
325	3. **Downsampler reads raw, computes hourly summaries** because read-time aggregation over billions of rows is too slow. State: rollups exist.
326	4. **Summaries written back** to a separate retention tier. State: cheap reads enabled.
327	5. **Dashboard reads latest tile from Redis** (~200/s misses fall through to step 6). State: none.
328	6. **History range read hits the TSDB**, served from rollups for wide ranges. State: none. Risk: huge range → capped/paginated via cursor.
329	
330	> 💬 "Latest state lives in Redis, history lives in the time-series store, and a downsampler keeps both cheap — I never scan raw points to render a dashboard."
331	
332	> 🎙️ Script: "Raw samples land in a time-series database partitioned by day; the latest value per machine is mirrored into Redis. A downsampler turns raw points into hourly and daily summaries so dashboards and billing read summaries, not the firehose. The trade-off is a few minutes of rollup lag, which is invisible behind a one-minute freshness target."
333	
334	### 6.3 "Bill lessees (lease + usage)"
335	
336	Money. Must be exact and idempotent, and it's a nightly batch — off the hot path.
337	
338	**Architecture decisions table:**
339	
340	| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
341	|---|---|---|---|---|
342	| Billing Service | A batch job that, per cycle, reads lease terms + usage rollups and emits one invoice per lease | Billing is periodic and deterministic; a batch keyed by period is naturally idempotent | Not real-time per-vend (out of scope; adds a transactional ledger); not hand-run SQL (error-prone, no audit) | Bills are monthly, not live |
343	| Billing Ledger (SQL) | Postgres — a relational database with ACID transactions storing leases, invoices, line items | Money needs strong consistency and an auditable, joinable record; invoices are low-volume and relational | Not a TSDB (wrong shape, no transactions); not Dynamo (conditional multi-row invoice logic is awkward) | Separate store from telemetry — we join across them in the job, not online |
344	| Usage Rollup | A per-lease aggregate of billable events (vends) over the period, from the downsampler | Pre-aggregating usage means the billing job reads small summaries, not billions of raw events | Not summing raw events at billing time (slow, re-scans everything) | Rollups must be complete before billing runs — we gate on a watermark |
345	
346	> 🎙️ Narration: "Billing is a nightly, then monthly, batch. I keep leases and invoices in Postgres because this is money — I want ACID transactions and an auditable, joinable ledger. The billing job reads each lease's terms and its pre-computed usage rollup, then writes exactly one invoice keyed by lease plus period, so re-running the job is safe. I rejected a real-time ledger because per-vend billing is out of scope, and I rejected summing raw events at billing time because that re-scans billions of rows every month."
347	
348	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
349	> **Postgres (ACID)** — a relational database that guarantees a multi-step write either fully commits or fully rolls back. Here it holds the money — leases and invoices — so a bill is never half-written.
350	> **Idempotent batch** — a job that produces the same result no matter how many times it runs. Here billing is keyed by (lease, period) so a retry overwrites rather than duplicates.
351	> **Watermark** — a marker meaning "all data up to time T has arrived." Here billing waits for the usage rollup watermark so late telemetry isn't missed.
352	
353	**Billing flow:**
354	```mermaid
355	flowchart TD
356	    SCH[Billing Scheduler]:::svc
357	    SCH -->|"1 trigger run period"| BS[Billing Service]:::svc
358	    BS -->|"2 read usage rollups"| TS[(Time Series Store)]:::store
359	    BS -->|"3 read lease terms"| PG[(Billing Ledger)]:::store
360	    BS -->|"4 upsert invoice by lease and period"| PG
361	    BS -->|"5 charge request"| PAY[Payment Processor]:::ext
362	    PAY -->|"6 charge result"| BS
363	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
364	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
365	    classDef ext fill:#3a1d5c,stroke:#9b59b6,color:#e6edf3
366	```
367	↳ reuses existing: Time Series Store (from §6.2)
368	
369	**Numbered narration:**
370	1. **Scheduler triggers a run for a period** once the rollup watermark is reached. State: run queued. Risk: late telemetry → watermark gating waits for it.
371	2. **Billing reads usage rollups** (small summaries) from the TSDB. State: none.
372	3. **Reads lease terms** from Postgres. State: none.
373	4. **Upserts one invoice per (lease, period)** in a transaction. State: invoices committed exactly once. Risk: re-run → upsert overwrites, no duplicate.
374	5. **Issues the charge** to the external payment processor with an idempotency key = invoice_id. Risk: processor down → retry with same key, no double-charge.
375	6. **Records the charge result.** State: invoice marked PAID/FAILED.
376	
377	> 💬 "Billing is just an idempotent batch keyed by lease-and-period — re-running June can't ever produce a second June bill."
378	
379	> 🎙️ Script: "A scheduler kicks off the billing run once we're sure all the month's telemetry has landed. The job reads each lease's terms from Postgres and its usage summary from the time-series store, then writes exactly one invoice per lease and period in a transaction, and charges the payment processor with the invoice id as the idempotency key. Because everything's keyed, re-running the job is a no-op, which is exactly what you want for money."
380	
381	### 6.4 "Detect low-stock / faults and dispatch restock or repair"
382	
383	Turning telemetry into action — fast on real problems, quiet on noise.
384	
385	**Architecture decisions table:**
386	
387	| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
388	|---|---|---|---|---|
389	| Alert Engine | A stream consumer that evaluates rules (stock < threshold, fault present) against incoming events | Reads the same Kafka log as ingest, so alerts fire within seconds and never block ingest | Not polling the TSDB on a schedule (minutes of lag, heavy scans); not in-gateway (couples auth path to rule logic) | Rule changes need a deploy/config push |
390	| Dedup/Debounce Store | Redis with short TTLs holding "open fault for machine X" markers | Prevents a flapping sensor from spawning hundreds of tickets/pages | Not unconditional alerting (pages on-call to death); not per-rule custom code (un-maintainable) | A genuinely new fault within the debounce window is briefly suppressed (tunable) |
391	| Dispatch Service | Creates/updates WorkOrders and notifies crews | Owns the work-order lifecycle and routing to the right crew/region | Not alerting directly to crews (no tracking, no dedup, no audit) | One more service in the path |
392	
393	> 🎙️ Narration: "The alert engine reads the same firehose log, so it sees a fault within seconds without ever touching the database. Before it pages anyone, it checks Redis for an already-open marker for that machine and fault type, with a short time-to-live, so a flapping cooling sensor produces one ticket, not a hundred. When a rule genuinely fires, the dispatch service opens a work order and routes it to the right crew. I rejected polling the time-series store on a timer because that's both laggy and expensive, and I rejected alerting straight to crews because we'd lose dedup, tracking, and audit."
394	
395	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
396	> **Stream consumer** — a process that reacts to events as they flow through the log. Here it evaluates alert rules the moment telemetry arrives.
397	> **Debounce / dedup with TTL** — suppressing repeat signals for a short window using an auto-expiring marker. Here it stops a noisy sensor from flooding on-call.
398	> **WorkOrder lifecycle** — the states a ticket moves through (open → assigned → done). Here the dispatch service owns it so every dispatch is tracked.
399	
400	**Alerting and dispatch flow:**
401	```mermaid
402	flowchart TD
403	    K[[Event Log Kafka]]:::async
404	    K -->|"1 read events"| AL[Alert Engine]:::svc
405	    AL -->|"2 check open marker"| RD[(Dedup Store)]:::store

474	## 7. Data Model & Storage
475	
476	**PART A — Entity table:**
477	
478	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
479	|---|---|---|---|---|
480	| Machine | **machine_id (PK)**, lease_id, cert_fingerprint, config, firmware | Postgres (Operational) | machine_id | Strong |
481	| TelemetrySample | **(machine_id, captured_at) PK**, device_seq, stock, cash_cents, faults | Time-Series Store | **time + machine_id** | Eventual (at-least-once, deduped) |
482	| LatestState | **machine_id (key)**, last_sample, state, last_seen | Redis | machine_id | Eventual (rebuildable) |
483	| Lease | **lease_id (PK)**, lessee_id, machine_ids, monthly_fee, usage_rate, term | Postgres (Billing) | lease_id | Strong |
484	| Invoice | **(lease_id, period) PK**, line_items, total_cents, status | Postgres (Billing) | lease_id | Strong |
485	| WorkOrder | **wo_id (PK)**, machine_id, type, fault_type, status, crew_id | Postgres (Operational) | machine_id | Strong |
486	
487	**PART B — Storage decision cards:**
488	
489	**🗄️ Time-Series Store — used for: TelemetrySample, rollups, usage**
490	1. What it is: a database purpose-built for time-stamped data, partitioning automatically by time and compressing repetitive readings about tenfold.
491	2. Why we picked it: our access pattern is "write ~3K rows/s, read by (machine, time range)" over ~8.6B rows/month — a TSDB does exactly that with built-in retention and downsampling, and our 8 TB compresses cheaply.
492	3. What we considered and rejected:
493	   - **Postgres** — 8.6B rows/month bloats indexes; range scans over months degrade; no native time-partition lifecycle.
494	   - **Raw files on S3** — cheapest storage, but you can't do fast point or range queries; we'd hand-build an index.
495	   - **Cassandra** — works as a wide-column time store, but no built-in downsampling/retention, so we'd reinvent the rollup tooling.
496	4. Trade-off: less flexible for arbitrary joins/ad-hoc analytics — acceptable since our queries are fixed-shape.
497	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "History is a time-series database — we write a few thousand rows a second and read by machine and time window, and it compresses telemetry about tenfold, so billions of samples is only a few terabytes and a thousand dollars a month. Postgres would choke on the row count and S3 can't query it."
498	
499	**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Machine, Lease, Invoice, WorkOrder**
500	1. What it is: a relational database with ACID transactions — a multi-step write fully commits or fully rolls back.
501	2. Why we picked it: leases and invoices are money and must be strongly consistent, auditable, and joinable; volumes are low (hundreds of thousands of rows), so relational is ideal.
502	3. What we considered and rejected:
503	   - **DynamoDB** — great KV, but the invoice's multi-row, conditional logic and reporting joins are awkward; vendor lock-in.
504	   - **TSDB for everything** — wrong shape for transactional money data; no joins, no transactions.
505	   - **MySQL** — equivalent here; Postgres chosen for JSON support and ecosystem.
506	4. Trade-off: two stores (telemetry vs money) instead of one — we join across them in batch jobs, not online.
507	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Leases and invoices live in Postgres because it's money — I need transactions and an auditable ledger. The volume is tiny so there's no scaling worry; the only cost is keeping money and telemetry in separate stores, which is fine because billing joins them in a batch."
508	
509	**🗄️ Redis — used for: LatestState, alert dedup markers**
510	1. What it is: an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads.
511	2. Why we picked it: dashboards and alerting need "current state of machine X" instantly; serving that from the TSDB is wasteful.
512	3. What we considered and rejected:
513	   - **Query TSDB for latest point** — 200ms+ per tile and unnecessary load.
514	   - **In-process cache** — not shared across service instances, goes stale inconsistently.
515	4. Trade-off: cache can be lost or briefly stale — fine, it's fully rebuildable from the TSDB.
516	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Live state is in Redis keyed by machine, so a dashboard tile is an instant lookup. If Redis dies we just rebuild it from the time-series store — it's a cache, not a source of truth."
517	
518	**PART C — Per-operation consistency:**
519	
520	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
521	|---|---|---|---|
522	| Write telemetry sample | TSDB | Eventual (at-least-once, deduped) | Display tolerates seconds; dedup makes retries safe |
523	| Issue invoice | Postgres | Strong (transactional) | Money — exactly-once per (lease, period) |
524	| Open work order | Postgres | Strong | Must not duplicate or lose a fault ticket |
525	| Read latest state (dashboard) | Redis | Eventual | ~1 min freshness is the target |
526	| Read-your-own-writes (ops marks WO done) | Postgres | Strong | Operator must see their own update immediately — read from primary after write |
527	
528	**PART D — Data lifecycle (largest table: TelemetrySample):**
529	At ~8.6B rows/month, raw telemetry is by far the biggest table. We keep **13 months hot** (~8 TB, the §3 figure) so billing and year-over-year comparisons work, then reclaim via **time-partition drop** — each day/month is its own partition and we DROP the whole partition in O(1) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns indexes and never reclaims space cleanly). Cold data we must retain for audit goes to **S3/Glacier** as downsampled summaries. Without this, telemetry grows unbounded and the $1K/mo storage line creeps every month.
530	
531	> 🎙️ Script: "There are really two worlds: telemetry and money. Telemetry is billions of rows a month in a time-series database, partitioned by time so we just drop old partitions instantly. Money — leases and invoices — is a small Postgres database with strong transactions because I can't afford a half-written bill. Redis sits on top as a rebuildable cache of each machine's latest state. I considered putting everything in one store but the access patterns are opposite — append-heavy time-series versus transactional money — so two stores is the right call."

535	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
536	
537	🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the requirements and the bottleneck — the firehose must never drop a fault — and reason forward from there.
538	
539	### How do we ingest the telemetry firehose without ever dropping a fault, even under a reconnect storm?
540	
541	**#### Bad: gateway writes straight to the database**
542	🗣️ Plain words: every machine message goes directly into the database the moment it arrives.
543	Approach: the gateway receives a batch and inserts rows into the TSDB synchronously, returning 200 only after the write commits.
544	Why people try this: it's the simplest thing — one hop, no moving parts, strongly consistent reads immediately.
545	⚠️ What breaks: when a reconnect storm pushes **~10K msgs/s** at the database and the TSDB is mid-compaction or a node is slow, write latency climbs, the gateway's connections pile up, and machines time out. They retry, amplifying load, and we **drop faults** during the exact incident (a regional outage) when faults matter most.
546	🔁 What forces the upgrade: any downstream slowness becomes device-visible message loss — unacceptable for a fault.
547	
548	**#### Good: durable log buffer in front (Kafka)**
549	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it coupled the device's success to the database's health — so a slow DB lost messages. We break that by putting a durable buffer between accept and persist.
550	🗣️ Plain words: the gateway drops every message into a fast, durable inbox and answers immediately; a separate worker drains the inbox into the database at its own pace.
551	Approach: gateway appends the batch to a **Kafka** log (durable, replicated, ordered) and returns 202. A consumer drains it into the TSDB. The log absorbs the 10K/s spike; if the consumer falls behind, messages wait safely in the log instead of being dropped.
552	⚠️ What breaks: the log buffers, but if the consumer stays behind for hours, **consumer lag** grows unbounded and freshness slips far past one minute; and a truly massive sustained burst could fill the log's disk.
553	
554	```mermaid
555	flowchart TD
556	    M[Machine]:::client -->|"1 batch"| GW[Gateway]:::svc
557	    GW -->|"2 append 202"| K[[Kafka log]]:::async
558	    K -->|"3 drain"| TW[TSDB Writer]:::svc
559	    TW -->|"4 write"| TS[(TSDB)]:::store
560	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
561	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
562	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
563	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
564	```
565	🔁 What forces the upgrade: we need bounded behavior under sustained overload and prioritization so faults beat routine heartbeats.
566	
567	**#### Great: partitioned log + back-pressure + priority lanes**
568	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: an unbounded buffer with one undifferentiated stream — under sustained overload it just delays everything equally, including critical faults.
569	🗣️ Plain words: split the firehose into a "must-not-lose" fast lane for faults and vends and a "can-wait" lane for routine heartbeats, partition the log so we can scale consumers, and when things back up, slow down the routine lane first.
570	Approach:
571	- **Two topics**: `critical` (faults, vends, cash) and `routine` (heartbeats). Faults are processed first and are tiny in volume.
572	- **Partition by machine_id** so order is preserved per machine and we add consumer instances to clear lag.
573	- **Back-pressure / load-shedding**: monitor consumer lag and log disk; under overload, shed by *down-sampling routine heartbeats* (keep 1 in N) while the critical lane stays untouched. The gateway returns 503 with Retry-After only as a last resort, and machines hold messages in a local ring buffer.
574	- **Dedup** on (machine_id, device_seq) makes the at-least-once log safe.
575	
576	```mermaid
577	flowchart TD
578	    M[Machine]:::client -->|"1 batch"| GW[Gateway]:::svc
579	    GW -->|"2 fault vend"| KC[[Critical topic]]:::async
580	    GW -->|"3 heartbeat"| KR[[Routine topic]]:::async
581	    KC -->|"4 priority drain"| CW[Critical Writer]:::svc
582	    KR -->|"5 shed under load"| RW[Routine Writer]:::svc
583	    CW -->|"6 write"| TS[(TSDB)]:::store
584	    RW -->|"7 write"| TS
585	    RW -.->|"8 lag signal"| BP[Back-pressure ctl]:::svc
586	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
587	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
588	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
589	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
590	```
591	
592	🔢 Decision-forcing math: peak ~10K msgs/s, but faults are maybe **1% = ~100/s**. Even if routine heartbeats back up, the critical lane at ~100/s is trivial to keep current — so a fault is never delayed by the firehose. A single Kafka partition handles well over 10K msgs/s, so we don't even need many partitions at this scale; partitioning is for headroom and parallel replay.
593	
594	✅ Failure matrix:
595	| Scenario | What happens |
596	|---|---|
597	| TSDB slow | Messages wait in log; critical lane drains first; no loss |
598	| Consumer crash | Resumes from last committed offset; replays, dedup drops repeats |
599	| Reconnect storm | Log absorbs spike; routine lane sheds; critical lane current |
600	| Kafka node down | Replicated partitions; another broker leads; no loss |
601	| Sustained overload | Routine heartbeats downsampled; faults/vends untouched; gateway 503 last resort |
602	| Duplicate sample | Dropped by (machine_id, device_seq) dedup |
603	
604	> 🎙️ Script: "I put a durable Kafka log between the gateway and the database so a slow database can never lose a machine's message — the gateway just appends and returns 202. Then I split the stream into a critical lane for faults and vends and a routine lane for heartbeats, and partition by machine. Under overload I shed by downsampling routine heartbeats while the critical lane — only about a hundred a second — stays instant. So even in a regional reconnect storm, a fault is never delayed."
605	
606	> 🧠 If they ask "why not just scale the database to absorb the spike?": "I'd be paying for peak capacity that's idle 99% of the time, and I'd still couple device latency to DB health. The log decouples them and costs far less."
607	
608	### How do we detect an offline machine — the event that didn't happen?
609	
610	**#### Bad: scan all machines on a timer**
611	🗣️ Plain words: every few minutes, loop over every machine and check when we last heard from it.
612	Approach: a cron job queries last_seen for all 200K machines and flags stale ones.
613	Why people try this: simple and obvious.
614	⚠️ What breaks: scanning 200K rows every minute is wasteful, the detection lag equals the scan interval, and it doesn't scale as the fleet grows; a long scan also means a machine can be down for the full interval before we notice.
615	🔁 What forces the upgrade: we want per-machine timeliness without a full-fleet scan.
616	
617	**#### Good: per-machine expiring keys (heartbeat watchdog)**
618	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it polled the whole fleet to find the few that went quiet. Instead, let each machine's own silence trigger the alarm.
619	🗣️ Plain words: every heartbeat resets a per-machine timer; if a timer ever expires, that machine is offline.
620	Approach: on each sample, set a Redis key `alive:{machine_id}` with a TTL of ~3 missed beats (e.g. 3 min). Subscribe to **Redis key-expiration events** — when a key expires, that's the "offline" signal, fed to the alert engine. Absence becomes a concrete event.
621	⚠️ What breaks: Redis keyspace-expiry notifications are best-effort and can lag under load; a Redis failover could mass-expire keys and trigger a false "whole fleet offline" storm.
622	
623	```mermaid
624	flowchart TD
625	    TW[TSDB Writer]:::svc -->|"1 set alive TTL"| RD[(Redis TTL keys)]:::store
626	    RD -->|"2 key expired"| AL[Alert Engine]:::svc
627	    AL -->|"3 offline alert"| DP[Dispatch]:::svc
628	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
629	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
630	```
631	🔁 What forces the upgrade: we need this to survive Redis failover without a false-alarm storm and to distinguish "machine down" from "our system down."
632	
633	**#### Great: timer wheel + correlation guard**
634	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it trusted a single best-effort expiry mechanism with no guard against mass false positives.
635	🗣️ Plain words: keep precise per-machine deadlines, but before paging "machine offline," sanity-check that *our* ingest pipeline is healthy — if a whole region went quiet at once, that's our outage, not 50,000 broken machines.
636	Approach:
637	- A **timer wheel** (a ring of time buckets where each machine's next-deadline lands in a bucket) gives O(1) scheduling and precise per-machine expiry, persisted so a restart rebuilds from last_seen.
638	- **Correlation guard**: before firing offline alerts, check ingest health (are we receiving *any* traffic from this region/topic?). If a large fraction goes silent simultaneously, suppress per-machine offline alerts and raise ONE "ingest degraded" page instead.
639	- On Redis/region failover, the guard prevents a 50K-page storm.
640	
641	```mermaid
642	flowchart TD
643	    TW[TSDB Writer]:::svc -->|"1 reset deadline"| TWH[Timer Wheel]:::svc
644	    TWH -->|"2 deadline passed"| AL[Alert Engine]:::svc
645	    AL -->|"3 check ingest health"| CG[Correlation Guard]:::svc
646	    CG -->|"4 single machine offline"| DP[Dispatch]:::svc
647	    CG -.->|"5 mass silence ingest alert"| OPS[On-call]:::ext
648	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
649	    classDef ext fill:#3a1d5c,stroke:#9b59b6,color:#e6edf3
650	```
651	
652	🔢 Decision-forcing math: with a 3-minute deadline and per-minute beats, normal jitter (one missed beat) won't expire; it takes 3 misses. If >5% of a region expires within the same minute, that's ~10K machines at once — statistically impossible independently, so it's our outage → suppress and raise one page, not 10K.
653	
654	✅ Failure matrix:
655	| Scenario | What happens |
656	|---|---|
657	| One machine truly offline | Deadline passes, guard sees ingest healthy, single work order |
658	| Region cell outage | Mass silence, guard suppresses, one "ingest degraded" page |
659	| Redis/timer restart | Rebuild deadlines from last_seen in TSDB |
660	| Late heartbeat (jitter) | 3-beat grace absorbs it, no false alarm |
661	
662	> 🎙️ Script: "Detecting the event that didn't happen is the subtle one. I reset a per-machine deadline on every heartbeat using a timer wheel, so silence becomes a concrete expiry event instead of a full-fleet scan. The catch is mass false positives — if a region's tower drops, every machine goes quiet at once. So before paging, a correlation guard checks whether our own ingest is healthy; if thousands go silent together, that's our outage and we raise one alert, not fifty thousand."
663	
664	> 🧠 If they ask "why not just alert on every missed beat?": "Cellular links are flaky — single missed beats are normal. I require three misses, and I guard against correlated silence, so on-call only hears about real failures."
665	
666	### How do we keep billing exactly-once when telemetry arrives late?
667	
668	**#### Good: idempotent invoice keyed by (lease, period)**
669	🗣️ Plain words: the bill for June is one row identified by lease-and-June, so re-running June just overwrites it.
670	Approach: billing UPSERTs invoices keyed by (lease_id, period); the charge to the payment processor uses invoice_id as its idempotency key. Re-runs and retries are safe.
671	⚠️ What breaks: if billing runs *before* late telemetry arrives, the bill is wrong even though it's exactly-once — correctness, not duplication, is the risk.
672	🔁 What forces the upgrade: we must not bill on incomplete data.
673	
674	**#### Great: watermark-gated billing + reconciliation**
675	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it guaranteed one invoice but not a *complete* one. We gate the run on a completeness watermark.
676	🗣️ Plain words: don't compute June's bill until we're confident all of June's telemetry has landed; if a straggler arrives later, issue a small adjustment, never a re-bill.
677	Approach: the usage rollup advances a **watermark** = the timestamp before which all data is known-complete (based on max observed lag). Billing waits for the watermark to cross the period boundary plus a grace window. Truly late data after the invoice is issued generates a *credit/adjustment* line on the next cycle, not a rewrite — invoices are immutable once issued.
678	🔢 Decision-forcing math: if observed device clock skew + network lag is bounded at ~10 min, a 1-hour grace window after month-end covers essentially all stragglers; the residual (<0.01%) becomes a next-cycle adjustment.
679	
680	✅ Failure matrix:
681	| Scenario | What happens |
682	|---|---|
683	| Billing re-run | UPSERT on (lease, period) → same invoice, no dup |
684	| Payment retry | Same invoice_id idempotency key → no double-charge |
685	| Late telemetry pre-invoice | Watermark + grace waits for it |
686	| Late telemetry post-invoice | Next-cycle adjustment line, invoice immutable |
687	| Machine changed leases mid-month | Each sample attributed by captured_at to the owning lease |
688	
689	> 🎙️ Script: "Billing is keyed by lease and period, so re-running a month can only ever produce one invoice, and the charge uses the invoice id as an idempotency key so the processor can't double-charge. The subtler risk is completeness — telemetry can be late — so I gate the run on a watermark plus a grace window, and anything that trickles in after we've issued the bill becomes an adjustment next month rather than a rewrite."
690	
691	> 🧠 If they ask "what if a machine's clock is wrong?": "We attribute by the device's captured_at but cross-check against received time; implausible skew is clamped and flagged, and the bounded-lag watermark protects the run."

695	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
696	
697	**9A — Availability targets:**
698	- **Ingest path: 99.95%** (~4.4 hrs/year down). Mechanisms: stateless gateways behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically); Kafka with replicated partitions; machines hold a local ring buffer so brief outages lose nothing.
699	- **Dashboard path: 99.9%** (~8.7 hrs/year). Mechanisms: Redis with replicas, stateless API, graceful degradation to stale-but-readable.
700	- **Billing: not measured in uptime** but in correctness — it's batch; a delayed run is fine, a wrong run is not.
701	
702	**9B — Per-dependency failure table:**
703	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what USER sees) | Recovery |
704	|---|---|---|---|
705	| Ingest Gateway | Machines can't push | Machines buffer locally and retry; no loss for the outage window | Auto-scale/replace instances |
706	| Kafka log | Ingest stalls | Gateway 503, machines buffer; dashboards go stale | Replicated brokers, leader re-election |
707	| TSDB | No new history/queries | Dashboards serve last cached state (Redis); history reads fail with retry | Restore from replicas; consumer replays log |
708	| Redis cache | Slow live tiles | Dashboard falls back to TSDB latest-point (slower, still works) | Rebuild from TSDB |
709	| Billing Postgres | Billing/invoices unavailable | No user-facing impact (batch); ops sees delayed run | Failover to standby; re-run idempotent job |
710	| Payment processor | Charges fail | Invoice stays UNPAID, retried with idempotency key | Retry queue; manual fallback |
711	
712	**9C — RPO / RTO:**
713	- **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we can lose on a crash. **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until service is back.
714	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
715	|---|---|---|---|
716	| Invoices / leases (money) | ~0 | minutes | Synchronous replication, standby failover |
717	| Telemetry (TSDB) | seconds | minutes | Kafka replay rebuilds any gap from the log |
718	| Latest-state cache | n/a | seconds | Rebuilt from TSDB, never authoritative |
719	
720	**9D — Cost breakdown (from capacity.py):**
721	Top 3 drivers and why: (1) **Ingest + stream compute (~$8K/mo)** — gateways, Kafka brokers, and consumers run 24/7 to hold the firehose. (2) **TSDB query nodes + storage (~$6.8K/mo)** — compute to serve range queries and run rollups; storage itself is only ~$1K. (3) **Everything else (~$6K/mo)** — dashboards, billing, alerting, observability. Total **~$21K/mo**, **~$2.41 per 1M telemetry messages**. Biggest optimization: **edge aggregation** — if heartbeats grew, having machines pre-aggregate or send deltas would cut ingest and storage proportionally; today the win is downsampling aggressively so query nodes stay small.
722	
723	**Back-pressure** (hottest path = ingest): bounded log, lag-triggered shedding of routine heartbeats, critical lane protected (see §8). **Observability**: golden signals are ingest lag (saturation), accept p99 (latency), 202-rate (traffic), gateway 5xx and dedup-drop rate (errors); a **burn-rate alert** fires if consumer lag exceeds the 1-minute freshness budget; a **trace-id** is stamped at the gateway and propagated through Kafka headers into every consumer so one machine's message is traceable across the async hop. **Rate-limiting**: per-device token bucket at the gateway (a misbehaving machine spamming gets **429 + Retry-After**); the limiter state is itself a small Redis-backed coordinator.
724	
725	> 🎙️ Script: "If Kafka or the database hiccups, machines just buffer locally and dashboards go a little stale — nobody loses a fault, because the log replays everything. Money data is synchronously replicated so we lose nothing on a crash and fail over in minutes. The cost story is unusual: storage is almost free at a thousand a month; the real spend is the always-on compute holding the firehose, about twenty-one thousand a month, or roughly two and a half dollars per million messages."
726	
727	---
728	
729	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
730	
731	**🔀 Decision: durable log (Kafka) buffer vs direct database writes**
732	1. Chose: a durable log between accept and persist, because the firehose must never lose a fault.
733	2. Gave up: operational complexity of running Kafka and a few seconds of write latency.
734	3. 🗣️ Plain words: like a mailroom that signs for every package instantly and sorts them later, instead of making the delivery truck wait at each desk.
735	4. When this reverses: if heartbeats dropped to every 15 minutes and we accepted occasional loss, we'd write directly and drop Kafka.
736	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The log is the shock absorber — it's why a slow database can't lose a machine's fault."
737	
738	**🔀 Decision: time-series database vs general SQL for history**
739	1. Chose: a TSDB, because 8.6B rows/month with range queries is exactly its job.
740	2. Gave up: ad-hoc joins and SQL flexibility on telemetry.
741	3. 🗣️ Plain words: a specialized tool for timelines beats a general one buried under billions of rows.
742	4. When this reverses: if telemetry volume collapsed to millions of rows, plain Postgres would be simpler and we'd consolidate stores.
743	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Postgres would choke on the row count; a time-series store eats it and compresses it tenfold."
744	
745	**🔀 Decision: batch billing vs real-time per-vend ledger**
746	1. Chose: monthly idempotent batch, because lease billing is periodic and deterministic.
747	2. Gave up: real-time revenue visibility per sale.
748	3. 🗣️ Plain words: tally the month and send one bill, instead of ringing a register on every can sold.
749	4. When this reverses: if the product needed real-time per-vend billing, we'd add a strongly-consistent ledger off the vend stream.
750	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Billing is a safe monthly batch keyed by lease and period — re-running it can't double-charge."
751	
752	**CAP/PACELC framing:** the telemetry write path is **AP** — we favor availability and accept eventual consistency (at-least-once + dedup), because dropping a machine is worse than a few seconds of staleness. The billing/invoice path is **CP** — strongly consistent, we'd rather block than mis-bill. Under PACELC, telemetry favors **latency** in normal operation; billing favors **consistency**.
753	
754	> 🎙️ Script: "My three least-certain calls are the log buffer, the time-series store, and batch billing. The log adds ops complexity but it's the reason we never drop a fault. The TSDB gives up SQL flexibility but it's the only thing that eats billions of rows cheaply. Batch billing trades real-time visibility for exactly-once correctness. If the volume or the billing model changed, I'd revisit each — but for a leased fleet on per-minute heartbeats, these are right."
755	
756	---
757	
758	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
759	
760	**❓ Two telemetry messages arrive for the same reading — do we store it twice?**
761	Mechanism: each sample carries a device_seq (a monotonic counter the device sets) and captured_at; the TSDB writer dedups on (machine_id, device_seq), so an at-least-once retry is dropped before it's written.
762	🗣️ Plain words: every reading has a unique stamp, so a duplicate is recognized and ignored.
763	💬 "At-least-once delivery plus a per-device sequence number means retries are harmless."
764	
765	**❓ What happens during a reconnect storm when 10K machines come back at once?**
766	Mechanism: the Kafka log absorbs the spike; the critical lane (faults/vends, ~100/s) drains first while routine heartbeats can lag or be downsampled under back-pressure; the gateway returns 503 only as a last resort and machines buffer locally.
767	🗣️ Plain words: the inbox swallows the surge and we process the important stuff first.
768	💬 "The durable log eats the spike; faults never queue behind heartbeats."
769	
770	**❓ How do you know a machine is offline rather than just quiet?**
771	Mechanism: every heartbeat resets a per-machine deadline in a timer wheel; an expiry is the offline signal, but a correlation guard suppresses mass false positives by checking ingest health first.
772	🗣️ Plain words: silence past a grace window means offline, unless a whole region went quiet — then it's our outage.
773	💬 "Three missed beats means offline, but correlated silence means it's us, not them."
774	
775	**❓ Can the same lease be billed twice?**
776	Mechanism: invoices are keyed by (lease_id, period) and UPSERTed; charges use invoice_id as the payment idempotency key. Re-runs and retries converge on one invoice.
777	🗣️ Plain words: the June bill is one row, so running billing again just rewrites the same bill.
778	💬 "Billing is idempotent by lease and period — no double bills, ever."
779	
780	**❓ What if telemetry arrives after we've already billed the month?**
781	Mechanism: billing waits for a completeness watermark plus a grace window; stragglers after the invoice is issued become a credit/adjustment line next cycle — invoices are immutable.
782	🗣️ Plain words: we wait for late data, and anything later becomes an adjustment, not a re-bill.
783	💬 "Watermark-gated billing, with post-issue stragglers handled as adjustments."
784	
785	**❓ What if Redis crashes?**
786	Mechanism: Redis only holds latest-state and dedup markers — both rebuildable. Dashboards fall back to reading the latest point from the TSDB (slower); alert dedup briefly resets but the work-order unique constraint still prevents duplicate tickets.
787	🗣️ Plain words: the cache dying just makes things a bit slower; nothing is lost.
788	💬 "Redis is a rebuildable cache — losing it degrades speed, not correctness."
789	
790	**❓ What if the time-series store goes down?**
791	Mechanism: ingest keeps buffering in Kafka, so no telemetry is lost; new history and queries pause; on recovery the writer replays from its last offset. Dashboards serve last-cached state from Redis.
792	🗣️ Plain words: messages wait safely in the log and replay when the database is back.
793	💬 "The log is our DR buffer — the TSDB can be rebuilt by replaying it."
794	
795	**❓ What if the payment processor is down at billing time?**
796	Mechanism: invoices are still computed and stored as UNPAID; the charge is retried with the invoice_id idempotency key until it succeeds.
797	🗣️ Plain words: we still produce the bill and just retry the charge safely.
798	💬 "We decouple invoice creation from charging, so processor outages don't lose bills."
799	
800	**❓ How do you stop a machine from spoofing another's identity or faking data?**
801	Mechanism: each device authenticates with mutual TLS using a per-device certificate; machine_id is bound from the cert, never trusted from the body. Implausible values (negative stock, clock skew) are clamped and flagged.
802	🗣️ Plain words: a machine proves who it is with a certificate, so it can't pretend to be another.
803	💬 "Identity is from the cert, not the payload — the body can't lie about who it is."
804	
805	**❓ How do you prevent a flapping sensor from paging on-call a hundred times?**
806	Mechanism: the alert engine checks a Redis dedup marker keyed by (machine_id, fault_type) with a TTL before paging; while a work order is open, repeat signals refresh the marker instead of creating new tickets.
807	🗣️ Plain words: one open ticket per problem per machine, no matter how often it flaps.
808	💬 "Debounce with an open-marker TTL collapses noise into one ticket."
809	
810	**❓ What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?**
811	Mechanism: always-on ingest + stream compute (~$8K/mo) dominates, not storage (~$1K). The biggest lever is edge aggregation/deltas if volume grows, and aggressive downsampling to keep TSDB query nodes small.
812	🗣️ Plain words: the cost is the machinery that's always running to catch the firehose, not the disk.
813	💬 "Compute that holds the firehose is the cost; storage is a rounding error."
814	
815	**❓ How would you add real-time per-vend billing later?**
816	Mechanism: vend events already flow on the critical Kafka lane; we'd add a strongly-consistent ledger consumer that writes an append-only entry per vend with idempotent (machine_id, device_seq) keys, then the monthly invoice rolls up the ledger.
817	🗣️ Plain words: the data's already streaming, so we'd add a money-grade ledger reading it.
818	💬 "Vends are already on the stream — real-time billing is a new consumer, not a redesign."
819	
820	**❓ How would you scale from 200K to millions of machines or go global?**
821	Mechanism: shard ingest by region (regional gateways + regional Kafka), push aggregation toward the edge, and keep billing/ops in a home region. The TSDB partitions by machine, so it scales horizontally; the log partitions add consumer parallelism.
822	🗣️ Plain words: split the firehose by region and let each region handle its own.
823	💬 "Regional ingest shards plus edge aggregation take us to millions without touching the core."
824	
825	**❓ Can an operator see another operator's machines?**
826	Mechanism: every read enforces object-level authz — the JWT identifies the lessee and we check lease ownership of the requested machine/invoice, not just that they're logged in.
827	🗣️ Plain words: you only ever see machines on your own leases.
828	💬 "Authz is object-level — we check you own this lease, not just that you're authenticated."
829	
830	> 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "This is a fleet-scale system that swallows telemetry from thousands of leased vending machines and turns it into correct bills and timely repairs. The design splits into two halves. The read half is small and cheap — a Redis cache of each machine's latest state for live dashboards, and a time-series database holding billions of compressed samples for history, with a downsampler keeping range reads fast. The write half is the firehose — machines push through a dumb gateway into a durable Kafka log, and the hardest problem is never dropping a fault under a reconnect storm, which we solve by splitting the stream into a protected critical lane and a sheddable routine lane buffered behind the log. The two halves connect through that one log: every consumer — time-series writer, alerting, billing feed — reads the same durable stream independently. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: the firehose must never block and never drop, so we buffer first and process at our own pace."
831

import re
text=open('_response.md').read()
blocks=re.findall(r'```mermaid\n(.*?)```', text, re.S)
print("NUM MERMAID BLOCKS:", len(blocks))
# breaking chars in labels: parentheses, colon, semicolon, slash, quote, angle brackets, curly braces
break_chars=set('():;/<>{}"')
for i,b in enumerate(blocks):
    print(f"\n===== BLOCK {i+1} =====")
    for ln in b.splitlines():
        s=ln.strip()
        if not s: continue
        # find label/edge-text segments: inside [], (), {{}}, [[]], (()), and |...|
        # collect bracketed labels and pipe-quoted edge text
        labels=[]
        labels+=re.findall(r'\|([^|]*)\|', ln)            # edge text
        labels+=re.findall(r'\[\[(.*?)\]\]', ln)          # [[ ]]
        labels+=re.findall(r'\(\((.*?)\)\)', ln)          # (( ))
        labels+=re.findall(r'\[\((.*?)\)\]', ln)          # [( )]
        # plain [ ] not already matched
        for m in re.findall(r'(?<!\[)\[(?!\[)([^\[\]]*?)\](?!\])', ln):
            labels.append(m)
        for lab in labels:
            # strip surrounding quotes for the check report, but check raw too
            inner=lab
            if inner.startswith('"') and inner.endswith('"') and len(inner)>=2:
                core=inner[1:-1]
                bad=[c for c in core if c in break_chars]
                if bad:
                    print(f"  QUOTED-BUT-HAS-BREAK {bad}: {ln.strip()}")
            else:
                bad=[c for c in inner if c in break_chars]
                if bad:
                    print(f"  UNQUOTED-BREAK {bad} in label '{inner}': {ln.strip()}")
PY
